parsedContent.title: ORDINARY LEVEL NATIONAL EXAMINATIONS ACADEMIC YEAR 2023 – 2024 PHYSICS I
parsedContent.description: NATIONAL EXAMINATION AND SCHOOL INSPECTION AUTHORITY

sections.name: A
sections.title: SECTION A – COMPULSORY (55 MARKS)
sections.description: Attempt ALL questions in this section.

questions.text: Complete each sentence below using the most appropriate term from the box. Each term may be used once or not at all.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: A ................................ is a region at each end of a magnet where the magnetic field is strongest.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: The area around a magnet in which it can exert a magnetic force is called ................................

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: At around 80°C, a magnet will become ................................ permanently if exposed to this temperature for a prolonged period.

questions.correctAnswer: a) Magnetic pole, b) Magnetic field, c) Demagnetized

questions.text: Identify the answer choice that best completes each statement or answers each question.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: The communication channel refers to:

options.text: the physical location where communication takes place
the medium through which the message is transmitted

subQuestions.correctAnswer: iii) the medium through which the message is transmitted

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Which of the following cables transmits signals in the form of light?

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: The circuit shown in Figure 1 represents an amplifier circuit. The electronic components shown are:

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: Which of the following electronic components is a semiconductor device?

questions.correctAnswer: a) iii) the medium through which the message is transmitted, b) ii) Fiber optic cable, c) i) resistor, inductor, transistor, d) iv) Ordinary diode

questions.text: Complete the following statements by selecting the correct term from the given alternatives.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Which of the following is a fixed point about which a lever rotates or pivots?

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: The Mechanical Advantage of a simple machine is:

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: The Velocity Ratio of a simple machine is the ratio of the distance travelled by the ......... to the distance travelled by the ......... in the same machine.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: All vehicles move with the help of the simple machine called:

questions.correctAnswer: a) iii) Fulcrum, b) ii) load / effort, c) ii) effort; load, d) i) wheel and axle

questions.text: State whether each of the following statements is TRUE or FALSE.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: The pressure exerted by a static liquid increases linearly with increasing depth.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Ice floats on water because ice has a lower density than water.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: A force acting on a larger surface area of an object exerts a larger pressure.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: Relative density of a substance is the ratio of the density of water to the density of the substance.

questions.correctAnswer: a) TRUE, b) TRUE, c) FALSE, d) FALSE

questions.text: State whether each of the following statements is TRUE or FALSE.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: A car parked at the top of a hill possesses potential energy.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: A ball dropped from a height h towards the earth surface is an example of kinetic energy being converted into potential energy.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Geothermal energy is heat produced by burning natural gas.

questions.correctAnswer: a) TRUE, b) FALSE, c) FALSE

questions.text: Select the correct answer among the given alternatives.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: The diagram (Figure 2) shows the electric field pattern produced by two point electric charges A and B. Which row correctly indicates the nature of the charges A and B?

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: The amount of force exerted on a unit positive charge placed in an electric field is known as:

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: What is the magnitude of the electric potential V at a distance d from an electric charge Q?

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: Which one of the following is an application of Electrostatics?

questions.correctAnswer: a) iv) A = Negative, B = Positive, b) iii) electric field intensity, c) i) Q / (4πε₀d), d) iii) Paint spraying

questions.text: Match the physical quantities in Column A with their correct dimensional formulae in Column B.
questions.correctAnswer: Speed → [LT⁻¹], Density → [ML⁻³], Acceleration → [LT⁻²], Energy → [ML²T⁻²]

questions.text: Differentiate between a renewable and a non-renewable energy source.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Differentiate between a renewable and a non-renewable energy source.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Renewable energy sources can be naturally replenished in a short time and are available indefinitely (e.g., solar, wind). Non-renewable energy sources took millions of years to form and cannot be replaced once used (e.g., fossil fuels).

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Give ONE example of each of the following:

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: A renewable energy source:
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Any one of: solar energy, wind power, hydroelectric power, geothermal energy, biomass

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: A non-renewable energy source:
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Any one of: coal, petroleum/oil, natural gas, uranium/nuclear fuel, minerals
i) Any one of: solar energy, wind power, hydroelectric power, geothermal energy, biomass; ii) Any one of: coal, petroleum/oil, natural gas, uranium/nuclear fuel, minerals

questions.correctAnswer: a) Renewable energy sources can be naturally replenished in a short time and are available indefinitely (e.g., solar, wind). Non-renewable energy sources took millions of years to form and cannot be replaced once used (e.g., fossil fuels). b) i) Any one of: solar energy, wind power, hydroelectric power, geothermal energy, biomass; ii) Any one of: coal, petroleum/oil, natural gas, uranium/nuclear fuel, minerals

questions.text: You are given four statements:

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Which of the above statements is an example of instantaneous velocity?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Statement C: At t=1s, a runner is moving at 4 m/s west

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Justify your answer.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: It specifies both the speed (4 m/s), the direction (west), and the exact instant in time (t = 1 s). Instantaneous velocity requires speed, direction, and a specific point in time.

questions.correctAnswer: a) Statement C: At t=1s, a runner is moving at 4 m/s west; b) It specifies both the speed (4 m/s), the direction (west), and the exact instant in time (t = 1 s). Instantaneous velocity requires speed, direction, and a specific point in time.

questions.text: The following diagram (Figure 3) represents electricity generation, transmission, and distribution.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Why is the efficiency of a real transformer less than 100%?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Due to energy losses (heat, eddy currents, hysteresis)

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Explain why thicker electric wires are preferable for long-distance electricity transmission.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Thicker wires have lower resistance, reducing power loss

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Identify the type of transformers on the poles shown in Figure 3.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: State ONE reason why electricity is very important at home.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: For lighting, cooking, powering appliances, etc.

questions.correctAnswer: a) Due to energy losses (heat, eddy currents, hysteresis); b) Thicker wires reduce resistance; c) Step-down transformer; d) For lighting/cooking

questions.text: The diagram (Figure 4) shows a brick being pushed by a force F on an unsmooth horizontal table.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: The pushing force does not make the brick move. Explain why:

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: The brick does not move sideways:
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The pushing force is equal to or less than the friction force, so the net horizontal force is zero – the brick remains stationary.

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: The weight of the brick does not make it move downwards:
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The weight (downward force) is exactly balanced by the normal reaction force from the table surface (Newton's 3rd Law).
i) The pushing force is equal to or less than the friction force; ii) The weight is balanced by the normal reaction force

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: A different pushing force makes the brick slide across the table from rest. The resultant force Fᵣ is constant over time.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Does the linear momentum P of the brick increase, decrease, or remain constant? Explain.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Linear momentum P = mv increases, because the constant resultant force causes constant acceleration (Newton's 2nd Law: F=ma), increasing velocity over time.

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: State ONE thing that the sliding brick does to the unsmooth table surface.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The sliding brick generates heat (friction converts kinetic energy to thermal energy) and/or produces sound.
i) Linear momentum increases; ii) The brick generates heat and/or produces sound

questions.correctAnswer: a) i) The pushing force is equal to or less than the friction force; ii) The weight is balanced by the normal reaction force; b) i) Linear momentum increases; ii) The brick generates heat and/or produces sound

questions.text: A vehicle having rectilinear motion is moving with an initial velocity of 10 m/s and accelerates uniformly to 15 m/s over a distance of 125 m.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: What is the acceleration of the vehicle?

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: What is the time taken to cover this distance?

questions.correctAnswer: a) 0.5 m/s²; b) 10 s

questions.text: When an electric current of 4 A passes through a resistor for 10 minutes, 2.88 × 10⁴ J of heat are produced.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Convert the electric energy E = 2.88 × 10⁴ J into kilowatt-hours (kWh).

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Calculate the voltage across the resistor.

questions.text: Figure 6 shows an alternating current (AC) circuit comprising a pure capacitor and AC generator connected in series.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: If the frequency f of the electromotive force of the AC generator is increased, what will be the effect on:

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: The impedance of the electric circuit? Justify your answer.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Impedance decreases. For a capacitor, Z = Xₙ = 1/(2πfC). As f increases, Z decreases (inversely proportional).

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: The rms electric current flowing through this electric circuit? Justify your answer.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: rms current increases. Iᵣₘₛ = Vᵣₘₛ / Z = Vᵣₘₛ × 2πfC. As f increases, Iᵣₘₛ increases (directly proportional).
i) Impedance decreases; ii) rms current increases

questions.correctAnswer: a) i) Impedance decreases; ii) rms current increases

questions.text: What would happen to the Earth without the hydrosphere?
questions.correctAnswer: Without the hydrosphere: There would be no liquid water on Earth's surface. Living cells could not carry out normal functions – all life would cease to exist. There would be no habitat for aquatic organisms (fish, crocodiles, etc.). No evaporation, so no clouds could form, meaning no rainfall and no precipitation. Desertification would cover the entire planet. The climate regulation provided by oceans would be lost, leading to extreme temperature variations.

sections.name: B
sections.title: SECTION B – ATTEMPT ANY THREE QUESTIONS (30 MARKS)
sections.description: Attempt any THREE (3) questions from this section.

questions.text: When light passes through a lens, it is refracted at both faces of the lens.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Copy the diagram (Figure 7) into your answer booklet and complete the path of the light ray through the section of the lens. Include the normal line at both faces.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of a virtual image in a converging lens.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: A converging lens of focal length 25 cm and a diverging lens of focal length -10 cm are placed in contact.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Calculate the power of the converging lens.

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Calculate the power of the diverging lens.

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: Calculate the power of the combination of these lenses.

subQuestions.label: iv)
subQuestions.text: Is the system formed by this combination converging or diverging? Explain by ignoring the lens thickness.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The combination is diverging because the total power is negative (-6 D).
i) +4 D; ii) -10 D; iii) -6 D; iv) The combination is diverging

questions.correctAnswer: a) Completed diagram; b) Ray diagram; c) i) +4 D; ii) -10 D; iii) -6 D; iv) The combination is diverging

questions.text: You are provided with the following electrical circuit components: connecting wires, 1 ammeter, 1 voltmeter, 1 resistor, 1 rheostat, 2 dry cells, and 1 switch/plug key.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Draw an electric circuit diagram showing the arrangement of the given laboratory materials.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: State the procedures to be followed and the measurements to be taken.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Analyse the data collected during measurements.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: State at least TWO safety precautions to be taken during the experiment.

questions.correctAnswer: a) Circuit diagram; b) Procedures and measurements; c) Data analysis; d) Safety precautions

questions.text: An ideal gas is a hypothetical gas whose molecules occupy negligible space and have no interactions, and which consequently obeys the gas laws exactly.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Draw a graph that illustrates Boyle's Law of ideal gas.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: A balloon has a volume of 2,500.0 ml of helium on a day when the temperature is 30.0 °C.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Convert 2,500.0 ml to litres (L).

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Convert 30.0 °C to Kelvin (K).

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: If the temperature at night falls to 283.0 K, what will be the new volume of the balloon if the pressure remains constant?

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: A pure helium gas is contained in a leak-proof cylinder with a movable piston.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: A sample of gas in a rigid container has a pressure of 1.40 atm.

questions.correctAnswer: a) Graph; b) i) 2.5 L; ii) 303 K; iii) 2.34 L; c) Final temperature; d) Initial temperature

questions.text: Archimedes' Principle states that a body immersed in a fluid experiences an upthrust (buoyant force) and this is fundamental to the equilibrium of a body floating in motionless water.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Consider a massless balloon filled with 10⁻³ m³ (1000 cm³) of water in a container of water.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: What is the volume of water displaced by the balloon?

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Show that the weight of the water displaced in the container is 9.81 N.

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: What is the buoyant force acting on the balloon? Explain using the figure that shows the body floating completely submerged just below the surface of the liquid.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Buoyant force = 9.81 N. The balloon floats completely submerged because the buoyant force equals the weight of water displaced, which equals the weight of the balloon contents (water), so the net force is zero.
i) 10⁻³ m³; ii) 9.81 N; iii) Buoyant force = 9.81 N

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Assume the balloon is replaced by a 0.5 kg piece of wood that has exactly the same volume (1000 cm³ = 10⁻³ m³).

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: What is the buoyant force acting on the piece of wood just after it was released?

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: What is the weight of the piece of wood in air?

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: Just after releasing the piece of wood, will it float partially immersed, float completely submerged, or sink? Explain your answer.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The wood will float partially immersed. Buoyant force (9.81 N) > Weight (4.905 N), so net upward force exists. Wood rises until buoyant force equals its weight.
i) 9.81 N; ii) 4.905 N; iii) The wood will float partially immersed

questions.correctAnswer: a) i) 10⁻³ m³; ii) 9.81 N; iii) Buoyant force = 9.81 N; b) i) 9.81 N; ii) 4.905 N; iii) The wood will float partially immersed

questions.text: Thermal expansion is the tendency of matter to change in shape, volume, and area in response to a change in temperature.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: i) Identify the general mode of heat transfer in solids.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: ii) Identify the general mode of heat transfer in liquids.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Differentiate between heat and temperature.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Heat is a form of energy transferred due to a temperature difference, measured in Joules. Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles in a substance, measured in Kelvin or Celsius.

questions.correctAnswer: a) i) Conduction; b) ii) Convection; c) Heat vs. temperature

sections.name: C
sections.title: SECTION C – COMPULSORY (15 MARKS)
sections.description: Attempt the following question. It is compulsory.

questions.text: In an experiment to investigate the relationship between the force on an object and the acceleration it experiences, a learner used the experimental set-up shown in Figure 10.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Identify the measuring instrument used to measure the following physical quantities:

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Mass:

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Distance:

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Provide a formula used to determine the acceleration of the cart. Justify your answer.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: a = F/m (Newton's Second Law). Since mass is constant in this experiment, acceleration is directly proportional to force.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Plot a graph of the force F applied to the body (y-axis) against its acceleration a (x-axis). Draw the best-fit line on your graph paper.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: i) What does your graph tell you about the relationship between the acceleration of the body and the force applied to it? Support your answer.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The graph shows that force is directly proportional to acceleration (linear relationship through origin). As the acceleration increases, the force applied also increases proportionally.

subQuestions.label: e)
subQuestions.text: On a trial run of this experiment, a learner found that the graph did go through all plotted points. Suggest ONE reason for this.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The experiment was performed under ideal/controlled conditions, the measurements were very precise, or systematic/random errors were minimized.

questions.correctAnswer: a) i) Balance; ii) Metre rule; b) a = F/m; c) Graph; d) i) Force is directly proportional to acceleration; e) Ideal conditions
